text:
In this study, the researcher investigated personal and social attitude of Maduranese people towards *carok* a violent ritual of conflict resolvement. And the factors that caused the continuation of this ritual. Using a descriptive method, the researcher collected data from 195 Maduranese people as respondents by doing field observation, questionnaire and interviews in four regencies of Madura. The results of the study showed that there was a difference between the common views of foreigner towards Maduranese people and the real attitudes of the Maduranese people towards the *carok* practice. From the individual interviews, 75% of the respondents did not intend to carry out the *carok*; 60% of the respondents said they would not *carok* for any reason, 77.38% of the respondents agreed that disputes should be settled through wise mediation, and 77.40% of the respondents agreed that the state and religion laws were sufficient to prevent them from carrying out the *carok*.

Most members (64.16%) of the community did not consider carok to be an important tradition in Madura and 81.11% thought that their community was peace loving. A majority of 86.11% did not think that carok was an important custom of the Madura community as a whole and 82.44% regarded it as immoral and also illegal. On the other hand, most members of the community had motives to perform carok—Honour Motives: In terms of frequency of mention, the honour motives concerning women were the most commonly cited; next in order of frequency were honour motives concerned with miscommunication of some sort, with regards to land and inheritance, with religion and with theft and debt. Conclusion The carok trigger as well as its defensive aspect of self-esteem are seen to be existing but the cultural impact of carok in Madura is seen to be declining in line with the general progress of the community towards more formal, modem education and towards higher levels of religiosity.
headline: Fully Human Written
prediction: We believe that this document is fully human-written
